right around now in the span of less

neither of these people are wrong but
they're also not completely right this
cute little human is my almost two year

I feel in-between and sometimes outside

sometimes I get surd and ma'am in the

Cheerios but this in-between land is

where I can be both a sir and a man
feels the most right in the most
authentic but it doesn't mean that these

trust me that discomfort can range from

unsafe at the time at a bar in college
when a bouncer physically removing by
the back of the neck is threw me out of
a woman's restroom but for me
authenticity doesn't mean comfortable it
means managing and negotiating the

until my experience as a trans person
collided with my new identity as a
parent that I understood the depth of my
vulnerabilities and how they were
preventing me from being my most
authentic self now for most people what
the child will call them is not
something that they gave much thought to
outside of culturally specific words or

mommy or daddy Papa but for me the
possibility is with this child who will
grow to be a teenager and then a
real-life adult will call me for the
rest of our lives with us was both
extremely scary and exciting and I spent
nine months wrestling with a reality
that being called mama or something like
it didn't feel like me at all and no
matter how many times their versions of
mom that I tried it always felt forced
and deeply uncomfortable I knew being
called mom or mommy would be easier to
digest for most people the idea of
having two moms is not super novel
especially where we live so I tried
other words and when I played around
with daddy it felt better better but not

that you really liked but you needed to

a lot more uncomfortable moments but
before I knew it the time had come and

daddy and I knew family face the world
now one of the most common things that
happens when people meet us is for
people to mom me and when I get moms
there are several ways that interaction
can go and I've drawn this math to help
help illustrate my options so option one
is to ignore the assumption and allow
folks to continue to refer to me as mom
which is not awkward for the other party
but it's typically really awkward for us
and it usually causes me to restrict my
interaction with those people option one
option two is to stop and correct them
and say something like actually I'm
Elliot's dad or Lda calls me daddy and
when I do this one or two of the
following things happen folks take it in
stride and say something like oh okay
then move on or they respond by pologize
it profusely because they feel bad or
awkward or guilty or weird but more
often what happens is folks get really
confused and look up with an intense
look and say something like does this
mean you want to transition do you want
to be a man or say things like how can
she be a father only men can be dads
well option one is oftentimes the easier
route option two is always the more
authentic one in all of these scenarios
involve a level discomfort even in the
best case and I'll say that over time my
ability to navigate this complicated map
has gotten easier but the discomfort is
still there now I won't stand here and
pretend like I've mastered this it's
pretty far from it and there are days
when I still allow option one to take
place because option two is just too
hard or too risky
there's no way to tell that or to be

so I weigh the I weigh the costs against
the risks and sometimes the safety of my

but despite this risk
I know as Elliott gets older and grows
into her consciousness consciousness and
language skill
if I don't correct people she will I
don't want my fears and insecurities to

to model agency authenticity and

soon after my mom showed up at our house

exaggerating it was like this high and
it was filled and overflowing with pink

annoyed to be confronted with a lot of
pink things and having studied gender
and spent countless hours teaching about
it in the workshops and classrooms I

sexism is a devaluing of the feminine
and how it manifests both explicitly and

aversion to a bag full of pink stuff
forced to force me to explore my

reinforcing sexism and the cultural

how much I believe in gender neutrality
in theory in practice the absence of
femininity is not neutrality its
masculinity if I only dress my baby in

world doesn't think oh that's a cute
Jenner neutral baby they think oh what a
cute boy
so my theoretical understanding of
gender and my parenting world collided
hard yes I want a diversity of colors

I want a balanced environment for her to

we even picked a gender-neutral name for

neutrality is much easier as a

as a practice and in my attempts to

inadvertently pillaging masculinity over

so rather than toning down or

we have pinks among the variety of

Elliot made me rethink and evaluate how

doing this self work meant I had to

hips my voice and I've made that
sometimes hard sometimes easy decision
to not take hormones or have any
surgeries to change it to make myself

and while I certainly haven't overcome

or damage how my kid can see the

feminine and female parts if I hate Arum
uncomfortable with my body how can I
expect my kid to love hers now it would
be easier for me to choose option one
to ignore my kid when she asked me about

Becky shaking your head no and mouthing

this went back and forth and total

she could have put the response

unfortunately we live in a world that

and my hope is that when confronted with

else we all take action like Sarah even
when there's risk involved so some days
the risks of being a genderqueer dad
feels too much and deciding to be a dad
has been really hard and I'm sure it

despite this challenge every day has

attaining and living a more meaningful
